coredata.dc:title: A wash-free lysosome targeting carbon dots for ultrafast imaging and monitoring cell apoptosis status
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Haoyue
Sun, Yuanqiang
Geng, Xin
Zhao, Keran
Meng, Hongmin
Yang, Ran
Qu, Lingbo
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized from N-methyl-1,2-phenylenediamine hydrochloride in ethanol solvent through the solvothermal method. The obtained carbon dots had no fluorescence in water, but could specifically light up in the cell which makes the staining process without washing. Interestingly, the imaging process can be performed by simply shaking the culture with the cells for only 1 min, indicating ultrafast and easy to operate fluorescence imaging. Moreover, the carbon dots with abundant amino functional groups had good lysosome targeting properties (the Pearson’s correlation coefficient is 0.92) and could achieve the ultrafast lysosome imaging in cell and zebrafish and monitoring cell apoptosis status. This is the first lysosome targeting carbon dots that allows the combination of a short incubation at the second-level with wash-free process providing great potential for continuous observation lysosome in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ultrafast
Wash-free
Lysosome
Cell apoptosis
Clathrin-mediated endocytosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization and optical properties of CDs
Ultrafast and wash-free lysosome imaging of CDs
Monitoring lysosome movement with chloroquine stimulation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and gaussian histogram of the CDs, (b) Full scan and (c–e) high resolution of XPS spectra of the CDs. (f) FTIR spectra of CDs. (g) UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission spectra of the CDs. (h) Fluorescence spectra of excitation-dependent of CDs (λex = 360–580 nm).

Colocalization imaging of HeLa cells incubated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 10 min and co-stained with 100 nM LysoTracker™ Deep Red (A), 100 nM MitoTracker™ Deep Red FM (B) and 2 drops mL−1 NucRed™ Live 647 (C) for 20 min (a1), (b1) and (c1) were confocal images of CDs (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–600 nm). (a2), (b2) and (c2) were confocal images of commercial dyes (λex = 638 nm, λem = 650–700 nm). (a3), (b3) and (c3) were merged images of CDs and commercial dyes. (a4), (b4) and (c4) were the correlation plots of CDs and commercial dyes intensities. (a5), (b5) and (c5) were the fluorescence intensity of the region of interest (ROIs) in the HeLa cells in the yellow and red channels. Scale bar: 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a–i) Confocal images of living HeLa cells before and after addition of 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 0–15 min (without washing). (j) The change curve of fluorescence intensity at different time-points. Scale bar: 25 μm. (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–600 nm).

Cellular uptake mechanism of CDs. (a) Confocal fluorescent images of living HeLa cells incubated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs at 37 °C, 4 °C and NaN3 for 2 h. (b) Average fluorescence intensities from the images shown in (a). (c) Effects of endocytosis inhibitors (5 μg mL−1 CPZ, 5 μg mL−1 MβCD, 10 μg mL−1 amiloride and 50 μg mL−1 genistein for 2 h) with 30 μg mL−1 CDs in HeLa cells analyzed by confocal microscopy, respectively. (d) Average fluorescence intensities from the images shown in (c). (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–600 nm). Scale bar: 25 μm.

Confocal fluorescence field images of living HeLa cells were pre-treated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 10 min and stimulated with 5 μM chloroquine (λex = 488 nm) for 0, 2, 5, 8 and 10 min. The fluorescent pictures of 0, 2, 5, 8 and 10 min with different stimulation time are showed with different colors. Two different time images were combined: (a–b) 0 and 2 min, (b–c) 2 and 5 min, (c–d) 5 and 8 min, (d–e) 8 and 10 min, and (a–e) 1 min and 10 min. Scale bar: 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence images with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 10 min of normal Hela cells (a)–(e) and (f)–(j) undergoing apoptotic by 40 μM dexamethasone for 60 min (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–600 nm). Bright field images with 30 μg mL−1 CDs of pre-apoptosis (k) and apoptosis for 60 min (l). (m) The change curve of fluorescence intensity of normal Hela cells and undergoing apoptotic at different time-points. Scale bar: 25 μm.
Confocal images of Zebrafish stained with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 0–15 min (without washing). (λex = 488 nm). Scale bar: 0.5 mm.
Zebrafish imaging of 30 μg mL−1 CDs at different growth stages (1-day, 2-day, 4-day and 6-day). Scale bar: 0.5 mm.
(a) The schematic diagram of preparation of CDs, (b) the schematic diagram of endocytosis, lysosome ultrafast and wash-free imaging and cell apoptosis of CDs.

head.ce:title: A wash-free lysosome targeting carbon dots for ultrafast imaging and monitoring cell apoptosis status

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized from N-methyl-1,2-phenylenediamine hydrochloride in ethanol solvent through the solvothermal method. The obtained carbon dots had no fluorescence in water, but could specifically light up in the cell which makes the staining process without washing. Interestingly, the imaging process can be performed by simply shaking the culture with the cells for only 1 min, indicating ultrafast and easy to operate fluorescence imaging. Moreover, the carbon dots with abundant amino functional groups had good lysosome targeting properties (the Pearson’s correlation coefficient is 0.92) and could achieve the ultrafast lysosome imaging in cell and zebrafish and monitoring cell apoptosis status. This is the first lysosome targeting carbon dots that allows the combination of a short incubation at the second-level with wash-free process providing great potential for continuous observation lysosome in vivo.

The obtained carbon dots can not only accumulate in lysosome rapidly by the clathrin-mediated endocytosis and were used for wash-free in lysosome and zebrafish imaging, but also monitor the dynamic change of lysosomal apoptosis.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Lysosomes, a subcellular organelle found in almost all eukaryotic cells containing many hydrolytic enzymes, plays important roles in many physiological processes such as intracellular transport, macromolecular circulation, endocytosis, apoptosis and autophagy [1–3]. Lysosome dysfunction is associated with many severe diseases, such as neurodegenerative disorders, lysosome storage diseases and several immune system diseases [4,5]. Therefore, monitoring the state and the changes of lysosomes is of great significance for studying their biological behaviors, functions, and gaining a better understanding of the diseases related to lysosomes.

Fluorescence imaging technology is a very powerful tool for monitoring lysosome biological process in vivo, which enables noninvasive real-time signals with high spatial resolution and sensitivity [6]. Designing and constructing various lysosomal targeted probes to meet the needs of cell imaging is a hot research topic at present. Some organic dyes [7] and photoluminescence nanomaterials [8] for lysosome fluorescence imaging have been developed. As one of the new types of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted growing attention due to chemical property and excellent photoluminescent properties such as water solubility, high stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and good luminescence characteristics [9–13]. For instance, Hosseini’s group prepared the green CDs for the detection of the Fe3+ and ascorbic acid, which can be used for intracellular imaging [14], Moassess’s group prepared a nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped CDs for highly sensitive and selective detection of traces of uranyl ion [15]. Lysosomes are the most acidic organelles (pH 4.5–5.5), so weakly basic lipophilic groups such as morpholine and amino groups can accumulate and enrich in lysosome due to the electrostatic attraction with lysosome [16,17]. However, CDs based fluorescent probe for the selective lysosome imaging is at its early stage, there is still much room for further improvement for lysosome targeted probes [18]. For example, the long incubation time for the probe entering into cell and targeting the lysosome and the rigorous, repeating and time-consuming washing process to alleviate the background signals from the probe unbounded to region of interest [19–22]. These two reasons make the cell imaging not only take too long time, but also inevitably alter the specimen environment and induce thermal stress and apoptosis, which make it difficult to monitor lysosome changes in real-time. In this case, it is essential requirement for designing novel lysosome targeting CDs with ultrafast imaging and wash-free properties.

In this work, N-methyl-1,2-phenylenediamine hydrochloride, a derivative of o-phenylenediamine, was used as precursors to synthesize CDs (see Scheme 1 ). The obtained CDs not only can achieve the goal of lysosome targeting, but also has excellent cell permeability and can target lysosome within 1 min. Moreover, the CDs had no photoluminescence in water but showed obvious fluorescence in cell which make the whole imaging procedure is wash-free. The CDs could also be used for lysosome imaging in cells and zebrafish and monitoring cell status during apoptosis, showing the potential application in lysosome studies and real-time monitoring.

N-Methyl-1,2-benzendiamine dihydrochloride was obtained from Aladdin Corporation and the ethanol (chromatographic Pure) was obtained from SayFo Technology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade hence they were used without further purification. All chemicals used are of analytical grade and don’t need to be purified.

0.05 g of N-methyl-1,2-benzendiamine dihydrochloride was firstly dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol solution, after then the reaction solution was transferred to the autoclave of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) inner bile which was heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Then the obtained products were purified via silica gel column chromatography (the eluent was ethyl acetate), and then dried further by rotary evaporator. Finally, the obtained CDs was dissolved in DMSO (10 mg mL−1) and stored at room temperature for further usage.

Cytotoxicity of CDs was carried out by CCK-8. Firstly, HeLa cells were inoculated into the 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h. Then DMEM was replaced with 100 μL new DMEM with CDs of different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μg mL−1). After further culturing for 24 h, 10 μL CCK-8 was added to incubate for 4 h, and the cell viability was carried out at 450 nm by SPARK multifunctional microporous plate (Tecan Austria GmbH).

HeLa cells were inoculated into the culture dish (1 mL per dish) per well and were incubated 24 h in the incubator (37 °C and 5% CO2). After then, the DMEM was replaced with fresh DMEM (1 mL) with CDs (30 μg mL−1) and incubated for 10 min. After three times washing, pictures were obtained by Leica laser scanning confocal microscope SP-8X (LSCM) at 488 nm excitation leading to an obtained emission spectral band between 500 and 600 nm. For the wash-free experimental study, cells were treated with CDs (30 μg mL−1) for 10 min. For the lysosomal colocalization study, the living cells were pre-treated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 10 min, then incubated with 100 nM lysosome commercial dye for 30 min, and fluorescence images were captured with LSCM at 638 nm excitation, and the collection wavelength ranged from 650 to 750 nm. For apoptotic experiments, the cells were pre-treated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 10 min, and then dexamethasone (50 μM) was added to monitor cell apoptosis.

HeLa cells were inoculated in confocal culture dishes at the density of 5 × 104 cells per well, and then cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. HeLa cells were pre-treated with 37 °C (control group), 4 °C and 10 mM NaN3 for 2 h, respectively, and then incubated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 10 min. After washing, fluorescence images of the cells were obtained using LSCM with excitation at 488 nm excitation leading to observe the emission spectral band in the range of 500–600 nm.

The endocytosis pathway of CDs was studied by endocytosis inhibitors. In order to block the endocytic pathway, four inhibitors, including chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPZ), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD), genistein and 5-(N, N-dimethyl)-amiloride hydrochloride (amilolide), were selected to study the endocytic pathway of CDs. HeLa cells were pretreated at 37 °C for 2 h with DMEM containing 5 μg mL−1 of CPZ, 50 μg mL−1 of genistein, 5 μg mL−1 of MβCD, and 10 μg mL−1 of amiloride, and further treated with CDs (30 μg mL−1) for 10 min. After washing, fluorescence pictures of the cells were obtained by LSCM.

Wild zebrafish embryos were purchased from GeneBio Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Zebrafish embryos were cultivated in the best breeding condition (28.5 °C). Zebrafish at different stages were incubated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs solution for 10 min, and were captured at 488 nm excitation using LSCM without washing steps. The collection wavelength ranges from 500 to 600 nm. All animal experiments approved by the Life Science Ethics Review Committee of Zhengzhou University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence CDs were prepared through the simple solvothermal method using the N-methyl-1,2-phenylenediamine hydrochloride in ethanol solution, and further purified by silica gel column chromatography. The size distribution and morphology of CDs were obtained by transmission electron microscopic (TEM). As can be seen from Fig. 1 a, the size of CDs mainly distributes between 1.1 and 2.3 nm with an average diameter of approximately 1.8 nm. And then X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyse chemical composition of CDs. The peaks of 284.8 eV, 532.0 eV and 398.7 eV are the C 1s, O 1s and N 1s in the full-scan XPS spectra (Fig. 1b). Then the three peaks of the C 1s region are attributed to sp2 carbons (284.8 eV, C–C/C=C), sp3 carbons (285.7 eV, C–N/C–O), as well as C=O (288.0 eV) respectively. Nitrogen atoms exist as pyridinic N (398.7 eV), amino N (399.4 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.24 eV). The two peaks of O 1s spectrum are C=O (532.0 eV) and C–O/C–O–C (532.5 eV). Furthermore, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1f) show that the N–H and O–H stretching vibration peaks can be found at 3370 cm−1 and 3431 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of C=C group is at 1614 cm−1. While the peak at 1322 cm−1 should originate from the stretching vibration of C–N = which means the cyclization between molecules, the band of C–O group can be found at 1147 cm−1. The results of the FTIR spectrum further confirmed the structure of CDs, indicating that CDs contain abundant amino groups, so CDs were used in the design of lysosome targeting.

Meanwhile, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrum and fluorescence spectra of the CDs were studied. UV–Vis spectrum shows the two absorption wavelengths of CDs at 380 nm and 430 nm, and the emission wavelength is 540 nm (Fig. 1g). Compared with the starting material, the absorption of the CDs shows a red shift from 290 nm to 380 nm and 430 nm (Fig. S1), which indicates that CDs should contain smaller electronic band gaps than the starting material [23]. Besides, unlike excitation dependent emission, the maximum emission of CDs is always 540 nm even if the excitation wavelength ranging from 360 nm to 480 nm (Fig. 1h), indicating that CDs has a stable uniform surface state distribution, which can lead to high quantum yield (Q.Y.) [24]. To confirm this, the Q.Y. of the obtained CDs was calculated to be 18% in ethanol solution by using coumarin 153 as reference. And the fluorescence lifetime of CDs is 31.6 ns (Fig. S2 and Table S2).

Then, the stabilities of CDs were studied. After 60 min of continuous irradiation with Xe lamp, the CDs still has 99% of its initial fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3), indicating good light stability of CDs in ethanol solvent. In addition, the fluorescence intensities of the CDs are almost unchanged in the presence of various cations, amino acid solutions, reactive oxygen species and different temperatures, indicating that CDs has excellent chemical stability (Fig. S4). The influence of pH was further studied (Fig. S5), the fluorescence intensity of CDs has a good linear relationship with pH between 2 and 6 with a correlation coefficient of 0.98, but remains stable in the alkaline range (pH 7–12), which could be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation process of the amino group present on the surface of the CDs [2,25,26].

Due to the excellent properties of the CDs, they were used for monitoring of lysosomes in living cells. After stained with CDs (0–50 μg mL−1) for 24 h, 48 h or 72 h, the cell viability remains at 92% when the concentration of CDs was 30 μg mL−1 (Fig. S6). The results indicate that CDs have low cytotoxicity. Besides, light stability is a key criterion for assessing the overall stability of photosensitive materials. As shown in Fig. S7, signal loss of CDs was only 15% after 60 scan cycles, showing good photo-stability. Low cytotoxicity and photostability suggest that CDs have great potential in cell applications.

To determine the distribution of CDs in cells, co-localization experiments with CDs and commercial dyes LysoTracker™ Deep Red (lysosomal commercial dyes), MitoTracker™ Deep Red FM (mitochondrial commercial dyes), NucRed™ Live 647 (nuclear commercial dyes) were carried out. The yellow fluorescence image of CDs overlapped well with the red fluorescence image of LysoTracker™ Deep Red (Fig. 2 A). The synchronization of region of interest (ROIs) of merged image was good as Pearson’s correlation coefficient was 0.92. On the contrary, the co-localization regions of CDs with MitoTracker™ Deep Red FM and NucRed™ Live 647 were significantly different in HeLa cells (Fig. 2B-C) and the Pearson’s correlation coefficient were only 0.62 and 0.59, respectively. These results clearly indicate that CDs have intrinsic lysosome targeting. The abundance of amino groups endowed the CDs with the ability to accumulate and enrich in lysosomes [27–29].

Next, the distribution of CDs was investigated in different cells lines (HepG2, HL-7702, RAW 264.7 and 3T3 cells). As can be seen from Fig. S8, the CDs could target lysosome regardless of the cell type, suggesting the highly universal and specific lysosome imaging of CDs. Besides, the staining property of CDs in fixed cells was then examined. Firstly, after pre-treated with 4% polyformaldehyde for 30 min, the cells were stained with CDs for 10 min. As shown in Fig. S9, CDs still has good lysosome targeting ability. The above results demonstrate that CDs have universal lysosome targeting for living, fixed and different types of cells, without further modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrafast and wash-free lysosome imaging of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ultrafast imaging is very important for monitoring lysosomal status and lysosomal diseases, so the permeability of CDs in living cells was investigated. Interestingly, the CDs (30 μg mL−1) could penetrate the cell membrane and accumulate in lysosome within 1 min, showing ultrafast lysosome staining of CDs (Fig. 3 ). On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity increased with incubation time until it reached a steady-state after 10 min. The ultrafast imaging is probably due to the small size of the CDs which enable CDs penetrate rapidly cell membrane. Furthermore, the concentration of 30 μg mL−1 was ideal for fluorescence imaging with negligible cytotoxicity (Fig. S10). Therefore, 30 μg mL−1 of CDs and 10 min of incubation time were selected as the optimal imaging conditions.

It is noteworthy that the above imaging experiment were directly captured without washing. There was virtually no background fluorescence, indicating the CDs have great potentials in wash-free imaging. To confirm it, HeLa cells pre-incubated with CDs were washed before their fluorescent images were captured. The fluorescence of the living cells and background after wash was similar to the wash-free one, indicating that the CDs can be used for wash-free imaging (Fig. S11). To explore the mechanism of wash-free imaging, the Q.Y. of CDs in different solvents with different polarity was studied. The Q.Y. of CDs in ethanol and methanol was very high, while in aqueous solution was only 1.5%. This is due to the intermolecular proton transfer between CDs and water which results in serious fluorescence quenching due to highly polar water molecules. The lysosome polarity is much weaker than water, thus the CDs should exhibit much brighter fluorescence in cells, and the background fluorescence of the CDs in culture medium is almost negligible [30,31]. Furthermore, due to the electrostatic effect of acidic lysosome, lipophilic amines are able to accumulate in lysosome up to 100-fold enrichment, which leads to more brightness of cells than background, which endows the CDs with wash-free imaging ability [32].

Considering the rapid uptake of CDs by cells, the possible uptake mechanism of CDs was studied in living cell to confirm the pathway of internalization. The effects of energy supply were firstly studied in cell uptake. HeLa cells were pre-treated with 4 °C and 10 mM NaN3 (which disrupts ATP production and blocks the endocytic pathway) for 2 h, respectively, and then incubated with 30 μg mL−1 CDs for 10 min. It’s clearly observed that the fluorescence intensities of the treated cells were significantly decreased compared to the control group (Fig. 4 a and b), suggesting the CDs the uptake is dependent on cellular energy [33–35].

Endocytosis is considered to be a general access mechanism of extracellular material, thus, chlorpromazine (CPZ), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD), genistein and amiloride, which are the inhibitors of clathrin-mediated endocytosis, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis, caveolae-mediated endocytosis, and micropinocytosis respectively, were used as endocytic inhibitors to study the endocytosis of CDs pathways [36,37]. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the fluorescence intensities of the cells treated with genistein, MβCD or amilolide were not significantly different from the control. However, the emission intensity of cell image was reduced to 70% after treatment with CPZ, indicating that the cell internalization of CDs was mainly affected by clathrin-mediated endocytosis, which is consistent with previous reports [38]. In conclusion, the cell internalization of CDs was energy-dependent and associated with clathrin-mediated endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring lysosome movement with chloroquine stimulation
ce:section.ce:para: The lysosomes in the cell are not stationary and usually move in the cytoplasm. Lysosomal motility plays an important role in many biological functions, such as endocytosis, apoptosis and autophagy [39]. Chloroquine, an antimalarial agent, can be captured into lysosomes and used for driving lysosomal movement as a weak base reagent [40–42]. To assess the tracking potential of CDs in lysosomal movement, chloroquine was selected to cause lysosomal migration without interfering in the normal function of cells in a short time. The cell images (Fig. 5 ) show that the lysosomes retain their original shape to a large extent at different time points, but their position changes point by point. The lysosomal migration of CDs was further demonstrated by combining images captured at different times to clearly record stochastic short period movements of lysosomal positions. The images quality of track lysosomes is high due to the negligible background fluorescence and bright emission of the lysosome.

It is well known that apoptosis will change the lysosomal pH, thus affecting the normal function of lysosome. Monitoring lysosome apoptosis is of great significance to the research and real-time monitoring of lysosome. Dexamethasone can induce apoptosis due to proton leakage the cells [43–45]. After 30 min of treatment with dexamethasone, the fluorescence intensity of CDs stained HeLa cells decreased by 65%, and the fluorescence signal of untreated HeLa cells remained 85% (Fig. 6 ). Moreover, it was clearly observed that the cell morphology changed from normal spindle to unhealthy round during apoptosis (Fig. 6k and l). The results proved that yellow fluorescent CDs can monitor lysosomal pH changes during apoptosis in real-time.

The zebrafish has proven to be a valuable vertebrate model for in vivo imaging in biological studies due to its fast growth time and optical transparency, hence zebrafish was selected for vivo imaging of CDs in this experiment [46–49]. The toxicity of CDs to zebrafish was tested. The zebrafish was soaked in 30 μg mL−1 CDs solution for 24 h, 48 h or 72 h, the zebrafish can still survive with good health status (Figs. S12 and S13), indicating the negligible cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility of CDs. Then the permeability of CDs was also studied in zebrafish. As shown in Fig. 7 , CDs can enter zebrafish by simple immersion through the chorionic membrane and the germ ring, and they are selectively accumulated in the eyes, head, yolk sac and tail of the zebrafish within 2 min, showing the different penetration ability of CDs for these tissues. These results show that CDs can quickly penetrate the cell membrane and enter the zebrafish body within 2 min, and has negligible background fluorescence, which further proves the conclusion in living cells.

Furthermore, zebrafish of different growth stages was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 , CDs was mainly deposited in the yolk sac in the 1-day zebrafish. As the zebrafish grows, CDs was redistributed in the yolk sac, tail and the eyes in 2-day, 4-day and 6-day zebrafish. It is also seen that the yolk sac of the zebrafish gradually becomes smaller in 4 and 6-day zebrafish than in 1-day zebrafish under the bright field. The above results indicate that the high biocompatibility of CDs has great application prospects.

In conclusion, a novel bright yellow fluorescent CDs was synthesized from N-methyl-1,2-phenylenediamine hydrochloride in ethanol solvent through solvothermal method. The obtained CDs not only has good selectivity to lysosomes needn’t further modification, but also has excellent cell permeability and could quickly penetrate the cell membrane into lysosomes in ultrashort time. Moreover, the CDs had no photoluminescence in water but showed obvious fluorescence in cell which make the whole imaging procedure is wash-free. The CDs can also be used to monitor cell status during apoptosis, which provides the possibility to monitor the dynamic location and morphological changes of cells in the physiological and pathological process for long time. This is the first lysosome targeting CDs that allows the combination of a short incubation at the second-level with a wash-free process, which can be used as a cell tracker for biomedical applications such as pathological evolution, biological process, and provides a new perspective for the research of biosensor.

Haoyue Qin: Writing - original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yuanqiang Sun: Writing - original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Xin Geng: Data curation, Software, Funding acquisition. Keran Zhao: Data curation, Software, Funding acquisition. Hongmin Meng: Visualization, Investigation. Ran Yang: Writing - original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Lingbo Qu: Visualization, Investigation. Zhaohui Li: Data curation, Software, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21708035, U1804136), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M620302, 2018T110736), the 2019 Youth Talents Promotion Project of Henan Province (2019HYTP003). We would like to thank Lijie Hou from the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for TEM analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An acidic pH independent piperazine–TPE AIEgen as a unique bioprobe for lysosome tracing
Chem. Sci.
